content.problemStatement:
You're working on a music streaming app where users often add songs to their playlists multiple times. Your job is to clean up a user's playlist by removing duplicate song titles, keeping only the first occurrence of each song — and you must use the Java Collections Framework to do this efficiently.

Given a list of song titles (strings), remove duplicates while preserving the original order, using Java Collections Framework classes.


Input Format

The first line contains an integer `N` — the number of songs in the playlist.
The next `N` lines each contain a song title (a string with no spaces).


Output Format

Print the cleaned-up playlist, one song title per line, in the original order, but with duplicates removed.
Titles contain only alphanumeric characters, no spaces.
constraints: Song titles consist only of letters and digits, length ≤ 100
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 10), the number of playlists. For each playlist, the first line contains an integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 10000), the number of songs. The next N lines each contain a song title – a non‑empty string of alphanumeric characters (no spaces), length ≤ 100.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the cleaned‑up playlist: one song title per line, in the original order, keeping only the first occurrence of each title. Test case outputs are concatenated directly, without blank lines between them.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The first occurrence of each song title is kept; later duplicates are ignored.
Only the first "Happy" is printed; the second and third are duplicates.
All titles are unique, so the output matches the input order.

solution.approach: Traverse the titles of each playlist from left to right. Keep a set that records titles already seen. When a title is read, check the set: if it is not present, add it to the set and output the title; otherwise ignore it. In Java the LinkedHashSet preserves insertion order, while in other languages a regular hash set together with immediate output works because we output only on first encounter. This yields linear time per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(total number of titles) – each title is processed once and set operations are O(1) on average.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(number of distinct titles in a test case) – the set stores at most N strings.

code.Python3:
import sys

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().splitlines()
    idx = 0
    T = int(data[idx]); idx += 1
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(data[idx]); idx += 1
        seen = set()
        for _ in range(N):
            title = data[idx]; idx += 1
            if title not in seen:
                seen.add(title)
                out.append(title)
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
